splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or whether actuaries may select assumptions after that date. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be determined by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that plan actuaries may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current data in withdrawal liability calculations.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts can apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the period of court-ordered conditions beyond what the judgment specifies when a person absconds. This affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year and raises questions about judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is tolled during absconding, allowing courts to revoke supervised release even after the original term would have expired.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release runs continuously regardless of absconding, and courts lack authority to extend the term beyond what the judgment specifies absent statutory authorization.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face a categorical bar under § 2244(b)(1) from raising any claim they previously presented in a successive § 2255 motion. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners are categorically barred; if not, they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255
positions.summary: Holds that the § 2244(b)(1) bar on previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, imposing an additional gatekeeping requirement beyond § 2255(h).

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bowe v. United States (cert case from 9th)

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions, meaning federal prisoners need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s own requirements for successive motions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to state election rules. The split concerns whether the mere competitive harm from allegedly unlawful ballot-counting procedures suffices for Article III standing or whether a more concrete injury must be shown.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates suffer cognizable competitive injury when state laws allegedly dilute lawful votes by counting invalid ballots, giving them Article III standing for pre-election challenges.

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that speculative pre-election competitive harm is insufficient to establish Article III standing to challenge ballot-counting procedures.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the rule of lenity trumps deference to agency or Sentencing Commission interpretations of ambiguous criminal provisions. At least three circuits hold that lenity prevails over deference, while others have held that deference to the Commission's commentary can override lenity. Post-Loper Bright, this tension has become even more salient.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over Auer/Kisor deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing guidelines, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through interpretive guidance.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that Auer/Kisor deference to the Sentencing Commission's commentary applies even in criminal sentencing contexts and can override the rule of lenity when the Commission has authoritatively interpreted an ambiguous Guideline.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), operates conjunctively (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify) or disjunctively (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' means 'and' or 'or' in § 3553(f)(1)'s three criminal-history criteria. This determines whether defendants must satisfy all three criteria to be excluded from safety-valve relief or whether any single criterion is disqualifying, significantly affecting mandatory minimum sentencing for drug offenses.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required to disqualify
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' conjunctively.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or.'

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which expands the definition of 'controlled substance offense' to include inchoate offenses like conspiracy, is a valid and binding interpretation of the Guidelines after Kisor v. Wilkie.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Sentencing Commission's Application Note 1 to § 4B1.2 — which adds conspiracy and other inchoate offenses to the career-offender definition — deserves controlling deference under Stinson/Kisor when the Guidelines text itself does not mention inchoate offenses. This directly affects career-offender designations and sentencing ranges for many federal defendants.

positions.label: Application Note 1 is valid and binding
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 reasonably interprets a genuinely ambiguous guideline and is entitled to controlling weight, so conspiracy qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under § 4B1.2.

positions.label: Application Note 1 is invalid — text is unambiguous
positions.summary: Holds that the text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, so Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the definition beyond its plain meaning and should not receive deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) may be considered as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law — alone or combined with other factors — as extraordinary and compelling reasons warranting a sentence reduction under the compassionate release statute. This split affects thousands of prisoners serving sentences that would be shorter under current law, particularly those with stacked § 924(c) sentences.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may constitute or contribute to extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, particularly when combined with other factors.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot be considered
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state schedule covers substances not listed under the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) is limited to substances controlled under federal law or extends to substances controlled only under state law. This split affects career-offender and other sentencing enhancement determinations for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: State-only substances qualify
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines even if the substance is not controlled under federal law, because the Guidelines text refers broadly to 'state law' offenses.

positions.label: Must match federal schedule
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the substance is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's property belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether property appreciation occurring between a Chapter 13 filing and a good-faith conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the bankruptcy estate or the debtor. This directly affects whether trustees can sell appreciated assets to satisfy creditor claims after conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion increases in equity inure to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion, based on the broad scope of § 541(a) and the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A).

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property belongs to the debtors, consistent with the Bankruptcy Code's structure, policies, and legislative history of § 348(f).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners must use habeas corpus (28 U.S.C. § 2241) rather than civil rights actions (Bivens or other statutory vehicles) to challenge BOP decisions affecting the conditions or terms of their confinement, such as denial of First Step Act time credits or transfer to prerelease custody.
splits.description: Circuits have been split for decades on whether challenges to conditions of confinement (as opposed to the fact or duration of confinement) may be brought as civil rights claims or must be channeled through habeas corpus. This split has intensified with First Step Act challenges to BOP decisions on earned time credits and prerelease custody transfers.

positions.label: Habeas is exclusive for duration-of-confinement claims
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to BOP decisions affecting when a prisoner is released, including First Step Act time-credit disputes, must be brought through habeas corpus and cannot proceed as civil rights actions.

positions.label: Civil rights actions also available for conditions challenges
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to conditions of confinement — as distinct from the fact or duration of confinement — may proceed as civil rights actions and are not limited to habeas corpus.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may impose sentences at revocation of supervised release that expressly punish defendants for the conduct underlying the violation, or whether revocation sentences must be limited to sanctioning the breach of trust.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the permissible purposes of a sentence imposed upon revocation of supervised release. Some circuits allow courts to punish the underlying violation conduct, while others hold revocation sentences should only address the breach of the court's trust. This affects hundreds of revocation proceedings annually.

positions.label: Punishment for violation conduct is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for the conduct underlying the supervised release violation when imposing revocation sentences.

positions.label: Revocation sanctions breach of trust only
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences should be calibrated to the breach of the court's trust, not to punish the underlying criminal conduct, which is addressed separately through new prosecution.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to appeals from orders denying attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment in criminal cases.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (subject to the 30-day civil appeal deadline) or part of the criminal case itself (subject to the 14-day criminal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion remains part of the criminal case, so the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause — barring states from enacting laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any . . . broker' — preempts common-law negligent hiring and selection claims against freight brokers arising from motor carrier accidents. This affects liability standards across the trucking industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers because such claims relate to broker services within the meaning of the statute.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted by the FAAAA because they do not regulate prices, routes, or services within the statute's meaning.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether FIFRA's preemption provision bars state tort claims that effectively require pesticide manufacturers to add warnings beyond what the EPA-approved label includes. This split affects product liability litigation involving widely used agricultural chemicals including Roundup.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because such claims impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims are not preempted by FIFRA because they run parallel to federal requirements and do not impose additional labeling obligations.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims resembling habeas challenges — such as alleged trial errors or sentencing miscalculations — may serve as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits permit consideration of such factors while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used as a substitute for habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider legal errors or changes in law affecting a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release cannot be used as a vehicle to challenge the validity of a conviction or sentence, which must be addressed through habeas proceedings.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to define the class of 'transportation workers' exempt from the FAA under Section 1, particularly whether last-mile delivery workers who transport goods across state lines or distribute goods that have traveled in interstate commerce qualify for the exemption.

positions.label: Broad reading — delivery workers qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods that have moved in interstate commerce, including last-mile distributors of baked goods, are transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow reading — delivery workers do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that last-mile delivery workers are not 'transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the FAA because they do not work in the transportation industry.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of liability and available remedies under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 and related statutes?
splits.description: The Second Circuit's approach to legal standards governing discrimination in hospitality establishments was found sufficiently unsettled to warrant Supreme Court review, suggesting a circuit split on the applicable standards for fair treatment, discrimination liability, and damages in the service industry context.

positions.label: Broader liability standards
positions.summary: Applies broader standards for establishing discrimination claims in hospitality settings, potentially allowing claims based on differential treatment without requiring proof of complete denial of service.

positions.label: Narrower liability standards
positions.summary: Applies narrower standards requiring more specific proof of intentional discrimination or complete denial of service to establish liability in hospitality discrimination claims.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal court filings containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether denial of a motion to seal medical information in court filings qualifies for immediate appeal under the collateral order doctrine as a matter 'too important' to be denied review and 'effectively unreviewable' after final judgment. The D.C. Circuit held that medical privacy qualifies as a value of 'high order' warranting immediate review.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal medical information are immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of high order that would be irreparably lost without immediate review.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that sealing orders generally are not immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because post-judgment remedies may adequately vindicate privacy interests.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
